title: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care

items.subtopic: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care
items.title: Understanding Neurologic Critical Care: Focus on Preservation and Prevention
items.content: Neurologic critical care aims to preserve neurologic tissue and prevent secondary brain injury. This is crucial in managing life-threatening neurological illnesses, whether they stem from primary disorders of the neuraxis or systemic conditions like hepatic failure, multisystem organ failure, or cardiac arrest.
keyPoints: Life-threatening neurologic illness can be due to primary neuraxis disorders or systemic issues (e.g., hepatic failure, multisystem organ failure, cardiac arrest).
Focus on preservation of neurologic tissue and prevention of secondary brain injury.
Secondary brain injuries include ischemia, hemorrhage, edema, herniation, and elevated intracranial pressure (ICP).
Encephalopathy describes diffuse, global, or multifocal brain dysfunction.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care

items.subtopic: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care
items.title: Identifying Encephalopathy: A Key Concept in Neurologic Dysfunction
items.content: Encephalopathy is a term used to describe diffuse, global, or multifocal brain dysfunction. It encompasses severe acute encephalopathies that arise from various neurologic or systemic etiologies but share the commonality of primary and secondary brain injury.
keyPoints: Encephalopathy refers to diffuse, global, or multifocal brain dysfunction.
Severe acute encephalopathies involve different neurologic or systemic causes.
Common themes in these disorders include primary and secondary brain injury.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care

items.subtopic: ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)
items.title: Understanding Brain Edema: Vasogenic vs. Cytotoxic
items.content: Brain edema, or swelling of brain tissue, is a critical concern following various types of brain injury and can be classified into vasogenic and cytotoxic types. Vasogenic edema occurs due to the breakdown of the blood-brain barrier (BBB), allowing fluid and solutes to enter the brain interstitium. This typically happens rapidly after an injury such as ischemia, trauma, infection, or metabolic derangements. Cytotoxic edema results from cellular damage leading to swelling, membrane breakdown, and cell death.
keyPoints: Vasogenic edema involves fluid influx due to BBB compromise.
Cytotoxic edema results from cellular injury and swelling.
Clinically significant brain edema often combines both vasogenic and cytotoxic components.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)
items.title: The Ischemic Cascade: Mechanisms of Cellular Injury
items.content: The ischemic cascade is initiated when there's inadequate delivery of oxygen and glucose, leading to a series of biochemical reactions. This includes the release of excitatory amino acids like glutamate, causing calcium and sodium influx that disrupts cellular homeostasis. Elevated intracellular calcium activates enzymes that damage cell membranes, resulting in cytotoxic edema and necrotic cell death.
keyPoints: Ischemic cascade begins with inadequate substrate delivery.
Glutamate release leads to calcium and sodium influx.
Cellular injury results in cytotoxic edema and necrosis.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)
items.title: Cerebral Perfusion and Autoregulation: Maintaining Brain Function
items.content: The brain maintains constant perfusion through autoregulation, adjusting cerebrovascular resistance to preserve cerebral blood flow (CBF) across varying systemic pressures. Cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP) is the driving force for circulation, calculated as mean arterial pressure minus intracranial pressure. Autoregulation ensures stable perfusion by vasodilation at low pressures and vasoconstriction at high pressures.
keyPoints: Cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP) = MAP - ICP.
Autoregulation maintains CBF across systemic pressure changes.
Vasodilation occurs at low blood pressure; vasoconstriction at high.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)
items.title: Mechanisms of Increased Intracranial Pressure (ICP) in Adults
items.content: In adults, the cranial contents include brain, CSF (~150 mL), and blood (~150 mL). The skull's limited capacity for additional volume means that any increase in these components can lead to elevated ICP. Obstruction of CSF outflow, cerebral edema, or increased volume from tumors or hematomas are primary causes of increased ICP.
keyPoints: ~150 mL of CSF contained within ventricles and surrounding brain/spinal cord
~150 mL cerebral blood volume in adults
Elevated ICP diminishes cerebral perfusion leading to tissue ischemia
Vicious cycle: Ischemia leads to vasodilation, increasing cerebral blood volume, further elevating ICP
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.subtopic: ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)
items.title: Consequences of Elevated Intracranial Pressure and Autoregulatory Mechanisms
items.content: Elevated ICP reduces cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP), leading to tissue ischemia. The body's autoregulatory mechanisms attempt to restore perfusion through vasodilation, but this increases cerebral blood volume, further elevating ICP and exacerbating ischemia. This cycle is prevalent in conditions like traumatic brain injury, massive intracerebral hemorrhage, and large hemispheric infarcts.
keyPoints: Elevated ICP lowers CPP
Ischemia leads to vasodilation via autoregulatory mechanisms
Vasodilation increases cerebral blood volume, worsening ICP
Cycle commonly seen in traumatic brain injury, massive intracerebral hemorrhage, large hemispheric infarcts
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.subtopic: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Severe Brain Dysfunction (part 1)
items.title: Differentiating Diffuse vs. Focal CNS Processes
items.content: In critically ill patients with severe brain dysfunction, it's crucial to rapidly distinguish between diffuse processes like metabolic encephalopathies and focal processes such as stroke or traumatic injury. This differentiation guides appropriate treatment strategies.
keyPoints: Diffuse processes include metabolic encephalopathies due to organ failure, drug overdose, or hypoxia-ischemia.
Focal processes encompass ischemic/hemorrhagic stroke and traumatic brain injuries with intracranial hematomas.
Prominent focal signs (e.g., pupillary asymmetry, hemiparesis) suggest structural lesions requiring urgent neuroimaging.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Severe Brain Dysfunction (part 1)

items.subtopic: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Severe Brain Dysfunction (part 1)
items.title: Indications for Urgent Neuroimaging
items.content: Patients with decreased consciousness and focal neurological signs, or those with coma of unknown etiology, should undergo urgent neuroimaging. CT is preferred initially due to its speed and ability to detect hemorrhage, hydrocephalus, and tissue shifts.
keyPoints: Urgent imaging for patients with decreased consciousness plus focal findings or unknown coma etiology.
CT scanning is typically the initial study of choice in critically ill patients.
MRI may be used for more specific information, such as acute ischemic stroke via DWI.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Severe Brain Dysfunction (part 1)

items.subtopic: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Severe Brain Dysfunction (part 1)
items.title: Role of EEG and LP in CNS Dysfunction
items.content: EEG is crucial for detecting nonconvulsive status epilepticus, while LP helps exclude infectious or inflammatory processes. Imaging should precede LP unless bacterial meningitis is suspected.
keyPoints: EEG reveals generalized slowing in metabolic encephalopathy and helps detect inapparent seizures.
LP may indicate cerebral venous sinus thrombosis if opening pressure is elevated.
Neuroimaging before LP is preferred, except when urgent treatment for bacterial meningitis is needed.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Severe Brain Dysfunction (part 1)

items.subtopic: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Severe Brain Dysfunction (part 2) | TREATMENT OF ELEVATED ICP
items.title: Indications for ICP Monitoring in Severe Brain Dysfunction
items.content: ICP monitoring is crucial for patients at significant risk of secondary brain injury due to elevated ICP and decreased CPP. It's particularly indicated for those with severe traumatic brain injury (GCS ≤ 8), large tissue shifts from strokes, hydrocephalus from SAH or intraventricular hemorrhage, and fulminant hepatic failure.
keyPoints: ICP monitoring is recommended for patients with GCS score ≤ 8 due to severe traumatic brain injury.
Patients with large tissue shifts from supratentorial ischemic or hemorrhagic stroke should be considered for ICP monitoring.
Hydrocephalus resulting from SAH, intraventricular hemorrhage, or posterior fossa stroke warrants ICP monitoring.
Fulminant hepatic failure is another condition where ICP monitoring can guide treatment with barbiturates or liver transplantation.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Severe Brain Dysfunction (part 2) | TREATMENT OF ELEVATED ICP

items.subtopic: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Severe Brain Dysfunction (part 2) | TREATMENT OF ELEVATED ICP
items.title: Management Goals for Elevated ICP
items.content: The primary goal in managing elevated ICP is to maintain ICP below 20 mmHg and CPP at or above 60 mmHg. This management strategy aims to prevent secondary ischemic brain injury by addressing the impaired intracranial compliance that characterizes severe cases.
keyPoints: ICP should be maintained at <20 mmHg to prevent secondary brain injury.
CPP should be maintained at ≥60 mmHg to ensure adequate cerebral perfusion.
Management of elevated ICP requires immediate attention due to the risk of significant increases in ICP from small volume changes.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Severe Brain Dysfunction (part 2) | TREATMENT OF ELEVATED ICP

items.subtopic: TABLE 318-2 Stepwise Approach to Treatment of Elevated Intracranial Pressure (ICP) $ ^{3} $
items.title: Goals and Initial Steps for Managing Elevated ICP
items.content: The primary goals in managing elevated intracranial pressure are maintaining an ICP below 20 mmHg and a cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP) of at least 60 mmHg. For sustained ICP levels above 20-25 mmHg, initial steps include elevating the head of the bed to midline position and draining cerebrospinal fluid via ventriculostomy if available.
keyPoints: Maintain ICP <20 mmHg
Ensure CPP ≥60 mmHg
Elevate head of the bed; maintain midline position
Drain CSF via ventriculostomy
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · TABLE 318-2 Stepwise Approach to Treatment of Elevated Intracranial Pressure (ICP) $ ^{3} $

items.subtopic: TABLE 318-2 Stepwise Approach to Treatment of Elevated Intracranial Pressure (ICP) $ ^{3} $
items.title: Osmotherapy and Glucocorticoid Use in ICP Management
items.content: For elevated ICP, osmotherapy with mannitol or hypertonic saline is used to reduce cerebral edema. Mannitol can be administered at doses of 25-100 g every 4 hours while maintaining serum osmolality below 320 mosmol. Glucocorticoids like dexamethasone are indicated for vasogenic edema from tumors or abscesses but should be avoided in cases of head trauma, ischemic, and hemorrhagic stroke.
keyPoints: Use mannitol 25-100 g q4h (serum osmolality <320 mosmol)
Consider hypertonic saline (30 mL, 23.4% NaCl bolus)
Dexamethasone 4 mg q6h for vasogenic edema
Avoid glucocorticoids in head trauma and stroke
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · TABLE 318-2 Stepwise Approach to Treatment of Elevated Intracranial Pressure (ICP) $ ^{3} $

items.subtopic: TABLE 318-2 Stepwise Approach to Treatment of Elevated Intracranial Pressure (ICP) $ ^{3} $
items.title: Advanced Interventions for Refractory Elevated ICP
items.content: In cases where elevated intracranial pressure is refractory to initial treatments, advanced interventions such as decompressive craniectomy, high-dose barbiturate therapy, and hypothermia may be considered. Decompressive craniectomy has been shown to improve outcomes in select patients.
keyPoints: Consider decompressive craniectomy for refractory ICP
High-dose barbiturates (e.g., pentobarbital coma)
Hypothermia to 33°C as a treatment option
Decompressive hemicraniectomy improves outcomes in select cases
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · TABLE 318-2 Stepwise Approach to Treatment of Elevated Intracranial Pressure (ICP) $ ^{3} $

items.subtopic: SECONDARY BRAIN INSULTS
items.title: Preventing Secondary Ischemia in Brain Injury Patients
items.content: Secondary ischemic brain injuries are critical determinants of poor outcomes following primary brain injuries. Effective management involves preventing episodes that exacerbate the ischemic cascade, such as hypotension and hypoxia.
keyPoints: Significant hypotensive events (systolic blood pressure <90 mmHg) for even 10 minutes can adversely influence outcomes after traumatic brain injury.
Hypoxia (pulse oximetry saturation <90%), especially with concurrent hypotension, leads to secondary brain injury.
Close monitoring of vital signs and early intervention are crucial to prevent secondary ischemia.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · SECONDARY BRAIN INSULTS

items.subtopic: SECONDARY BRAIN INSULTS
items.title: Managing Fever and Hyperglycemia in Brain Injury Patients
items.content: Fever and hyperglycemia exacerbate experimental ischemia and worsen clinical outcomes after stroke or head trauma. Aggressive control of these factors is essential.
keyPoints: Aggressive fever management aiming for normothermia can be challenging but necessary.
Use IV insulin infusion to maintain serum glucose levels at <10.0 mmol/L (<180 mg/dL).
Hypoglycemia is equally detrimental, indicating the need for careful regulation of glucose levels.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · SECONDARY BRAIN INSULTS

items.subtopic: HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 1)
items.title: Prognostic Indicators for Hypoxic-Ischemic Brain Injury
items.content: Assessing prognosis after hypoxic-ischemic brain injury involves evaluating clinical and electrophysiologic signs. Intact brainstem function, indicated by normal pupillary light responses and intact reflexes, suggests a better prognosis. Conversely, absent pupillary light reflex or extensor motor response to pain 5-7 days post-injury indicates a poor outcome, unless confounded by sedatives or hypothermia.
keyPoints: Intact brainstem function (normal pupillary light responses and intact oculocephalic, oculovestibular, corneal reflexes) suggests better prognosis.
Absent pupillary light reflex or extensor motor response to pain 5-7 days post-injury indicates poor outcome, except in cases with metabolic disturbances or high-dose sedatives/hypothermia.
Bilateral absence of N20 component of SSEP and nonreactive EEG are associated with poor prognosis.
Serum neuron-specific enolase (NSE) levels >60 µg/L within 1-3 days post-injury indicate worse outcome.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 1)

items.subtopic: HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 1)
items.title: Impact of Mild Hypothermia on Prognostication
items.content: Mild hypothermia treatment post-cardiac arrest can delay the reliability of clinical and electrophysiologic predictors for poor neurologic outcome. The false-positive rate for predicting poor outcome may be as high as 21% in patients treated with mild hypothermia exhibiting extensor posturing on day 3.
keyPoints: Mild hypothermia affects timing of reliable prognostic indicators.
False-positive rate for poor neurologic outcome prediction can reach 21% (95% CI 8-43%) in patients treated with mild hypothermia showing extensor posturing on day 3.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 1)

items.subtopic: HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 1)
items.title: Pathological Findings and Long-term Consequences
items.content: Hypoxic-ischemic brain injury results in extensive cortical injury, often affecting the hippocampus, basal ganglia, hypothalamus, and brainstem. This can lead to long-term consequences such as persistent coma, dementia, visual agnosia, parkinsonism, choreoathetosis, cerebellar ataxia, myoclonus, seizures, and an amnesic state.
keyPoints: Histologic findings include multifocal or diffuse laminar cortical injury with involvement of deep gray nuclei and hippocampus.
Hippocampal CA1 neurons are particularly vulnerable to brief hypoxia-ischemia.
Long-term consequences may include persistent coma, dementia, visual agnosia, parkinsonism, choreoathetosis, cerebellar ataxia, myoclonus, seizures, and an amnesic state.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 1)

items.subtopic: HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 2) | Hypoxic-Ischemic Brain Injury
items.title: Diagnosis of Hypoxic-Ischemic Brain Injury
items.content: Hypoxic-ischemic brain injury diagnosis hinges on identifying a history of hypoxia or ischemia, such as cardiac arrest. Key diagnostic thresholds include systolic blood pressure <70 mmHg and PaO2 <40 mmHg. Carbon monoxide intoxication can be confirmed by measuring carboxyhemoglobin levels.
keyPoints: Diagnosis based on history of hypoxic-ischemic event (e.g., cardiac arrest).
Blood pressure <70 mmHg systolic or PaO2 <40 mmHg are critical thresholds for cellular injury determination.
Carbon monoxide intoxication confirmed by carboxyhemoglobin measurement; cherry red venous blood and skin color as inconsistent indicators.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 2) | Hypoxic-Ischemic Brain Injury

items.subtopic: HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 2) | Hypoxic-Ischemic Brain Injury
items.title: Management of Hypothermia in Hypoxic-Ischemic Brain Injury
items.content: Mild hypothermia at 33°C for 12–24 hours post-resuscitation can improve outcomes, though results vary across studies. Current guidelines recommend maintaining a temperature between 32 and 37.5°C during postarrest care to avoid fever.
keyPoints: Mild hypothermia (33°C) for 12-24 h improved functional outcome in some trials.
Clinical trial variability; some found no benefit with TTM at 33 or 36°C.
Current guidelines: Maintain temperature between 32 and 37.5°C postarrest, avoid fever.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 2) | Hypoxic-Ischemic Brain Injury

items.subtopic: HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 2) | Hypoxic-Ischemic Brain Injury
items.title: Treatment Considerations for Posthypoxic Myoclonus and Encephalopathy
items.content: Posthypoxic myoclonus may respond to clonazepam or valproate. Delayed encephalopathy from carbon monoxide intoxication can manifest as a parkinsonian syndrome, with worsening symptoms over months.
keyPoints: Clonazepam (1.5–10 mg daily) or valproate (300–1200 mg divided doses) may treat posthypoxic myoclonus.
Delayed encephalopathy from carbon monoxide intoxication can develop into a parkinsonian syndrome with akinesia and rigidity.
Symptoms of delayed encephalopathy worsen over months, showing basal ganglia damage on CT/MRI.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 2) | Hypoxic-Ischemic Brain Injury

items.subtopic: POSTCARDIAC BYPASS BRAIN INJURY
items.title: Mechanisms and Clinical Presentation of Postcardiac Bypass Brain Injury
items.content: Postcardiac bypass brain injury can result from hypoperfusion or embolic disease. Modern techniques have reduced intraoperative hypoxic injuries, but some patients still experience complications due to cerebral hypoperfusion or focal ischemia. Emboli dislodged during surgery are a major cause of cerebral injury.
keyPoints: CNS injuries post-CABG include acute encephalopathy and stroke.
Hypoperfusion and embolic disease are key mechanisms.
Modern techniques reduce but do not eliminate hypoxic injuries.
Emboli from the heart or aortic arch during surgery cause cerebral injury.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · POSTCARDIAC BYPASS BRAIN INJURY

items.subtopic: POSTCARDIAC BYPASS BRAIN INJURY
items.title: Diagnosis and Clinical Syndromes of Postcardiac Bypass Brain Injury
items.content: Postoperative brain injuries manifest as acute encephalopathy or chronic cognitive impairment. Acute encephalopathy can present with hyperactive or hypoactive states, often misattributed to other causes. Chronic deficits may occur without an acute syndrome due to a lower burden of microemboli.
keyPoints: Acute encephalopathy presents as hyperactive or hypoactive confusional states.
Hypoactive states are frequently misdiagnosed as depression or sedative-induced delirium.
Chronic cognitive deficits can occur without acute symptoms due to lower microemboli burden.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · POSTCARDIAC BYPASS BRAIN INJURY

items.subtopic: METABOLIC ENCEPHALOPATHIES
items.title: Dexmedetomidine vs. Benzodiazepines in ICU Sedation: Impact on Delirium and Ventilation Duration
items.content: In critically ill patients requiring sedation, the use of dexmedetomidine may reduce delirium incidence and shorten mechanical ventilation duration compared to benzodiazepines like lorazepam or midazolam.
keyPoints: Dexmedetomidine is a centrally acting $ \alpha_{2} $ agonist.
It can reduce the occurrence of delirium in ICU patients.
Shortens the duration of mechanical ventilation compared to benzodiazepines.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · METABOLIC ENCEPHALOPATHIES

items.subtopic: METABOLIC ENCEPHALOPATHIES
items.title: Hypercarbic Encephalopathy: Clinical Presentation and Pathophysiology
items.content: Hypercarbic encephalopathy, often seen in patients with chronic CO2 retention receiving oxygen therapy, presents with symptoms ranging from headache to coma. It results from elevated Paco2 causing CO2 narcosis and cerebral vasodilation, potentially increasing intracranial pressure.
keyPoints: Symptoms include headache, confusion, stupor, or coma.
Occurs in patients with chronic $ CO_{2} $ retention on oxygen therapy.
Elevated Paco2 leads to CO2 narcosis and cerebral vasodilation.
Can result in increased intracranial pressure (ICP).
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · METABOLIC ENCEPHALOPATHIES

items.subtopic: SEPSIS-ASSOCIATED ENCEPHALOPATHY
items.title: Pathogenesis of Sepsis-Associated Encephalopathy
items.content: Sepsis-associated encephalopathy arises from the systemic inflammatory response to infectious agents, leading to circulating inflammatory mediators that contribute to brain dysfunction. This condition is not solely due to metabolic derangements but involves cytokines like tumor necrosis factor and interleukins (IL-1, IL-2, IL-6).
keyPoints: Systemic response to infectious agents releases inflammatory mediators.
Cytokines involved: tumor necrosis factor, IL-1, IL-2, IL-6.
Encephalopathy is not just due to metabolic derangements.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · SEPSIS-ASSOCIATED ENCEPHALOPATHY

items.subtopic: SEPSIS-ASSOCIATED ENCEPHALOPATHY
items.title: Diagnosis and Prognosis of Sepsis-Associated Encephalopathy
items.content: Sepsis-associated encephalopathy presents with diffuse brain dysfunction, including confusion, disorientation, agitation, and fluctuations in alertness. Diagnosis requires exclusion of other causes like structural or infectious issues. Mortality approaches 50% in severe cases due to underlying critical illness severity. Long-term cognitive dysfunction is increasingly recognized post-recovery.
keyPoints: Clinical presentation: confusion, disorientation, agitation, fluctuating alertness.
Diagnosis requires exclusion of other neurologic causes.
Mortality rate near 50% in severe cases reflects critical illness severity.
Long-term cognitive dysfunction similar to dementia is increasingly recognized.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · SEPSIS-ASSOCIATED ENCEPHALOPATHY

items.subtopic: OSMOTIC DEMYELINATION SYNDROME (CENTRAL PONTINE MYELINOLYSIS)
items.title: Gradual Correction of Hyponatremia to Prevent Osmotic Demyelination Syndrome
items.content: Osmotic demyelination syndrome, formerly known as central pontine myelinolysis, can occur after rapid correction of hyponatremia. It is characterized by demyelination in the pons without inflammation and may present with quadriplegia or pseudobulbar palsy.
keyPoints: Most cases are associated with rapid correction of hyponatremia or hyperosmolar states.
Clinical symptoms usually appear a few days after sodium correction.
MRI can help diagnose and identify partial forms presenting as confusion, dysarthria, or disturbances in conjugate gaze without quadriplegia.
Therapy should aim for gradual correction: ≤8 mmol/L within 24 hours and 15 mmol/L within 48 hours.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · OSMOTIC DEMYELINATION SYNDROME (CENTRAL PONTINE MYELINOLYSIS) | WERNICKE'S DISEASE

items.subtopic: WERNICKE'S DISEASE
items.title: Recognizing Wernicke's Disease in At-Risk Populations
items.content: Wernicke's disease is due to thiamine deficiency and commonly affects alcoholics. It presents with a triad of ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, and confusion, though only one-third present with all three symptoms.
keyPoints: Thiamine deficiency leads to Wernicke's disease, common in alcoholics and those with malnutrition or certain medical conditions.
Characteristic clinical triad: ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, global confusion; however, only one-third present with all three.
Untreated, it can progress to stupor, coma, and death.
Ocular motor abnormalities include horizontal nystagmus on lateral gaze and bilateral lateral rectus palsy.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · OSMOTIC DEMYELINATION SYNDROME (CENTRAL PONTINE MYELINOLYSIS) | WERNICKE'S DISEASE

items.subtopic: WERNICKE'S DISEASE
items.title: Thiamine Administration for Wernicke's Disease
items.content: Prompt administration of thiamine is crucial in treating Wernicke's disease, leading to rapid improvement in ocular palsies and gradual recovery from ataxia.
keyPoints: Ocular motor abnormalities improve within hours after thiamine administration.
Ataxia improves more slowly than ocular symptoms.
Approximately half of the patients recover incompletely with a slow, shuffling gait and inability to tandem walk.
Korsakoff's psychosis may persist, characterized by memory gaps, confabulation, and disordered temporal sequencing.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · OSMOTIC DEMYELINATION SYNDROME (CENTRAL PONTINE MYELINOLYSIS) | WERNICKE'S DISEASE

items.subtopic: Wernicke's Disease | HYPERPERFUSION DISORDERS (POSTERIOR REVERSIBLE ENCEPHALOPATHY SYNDROME)
items.title: Immediate Thiamine Administration in Wernicke's Disease
items.content: Administer high-dose thiamine immediately to prevent or treat Wernicke's disease, especially before glucose infusions.
keyPoints: Wernicke's disease is a medical emergency requiring immediate treatment.
High-dose thiamine (500 mg IV) should be administered prior to any IV glucose solutions.
Continue thiamine 3 times daily for 2–3 days, followed by 250 mg IV or IM daily for 5 more days with other B vitamins.
Switch to oral thiamine at 100 mg daily until the patient is no longer at risk.
Thiamine should be given to all alcoholic patients requiring parenteral glucose.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · Wernicke's Disease | HYPERPERFUSION DISORDERS (POSTERIOR REVERSIBLE ENCEPHALOPATHY SYNDROME)

items.subtopic: Wernicke's Disease | HYPERPERFUSION DISORDERS (POSTERIOR REVERSIBLE ENCEPHALOPATHY SYNDROME)
items.title: Pathogenesis and Presentation of Hyperperfusion Disorders
items.content: Hyperperfusion disorders result from endothelial dysfunction leading to vasogenic edema, primarily affecting the posterior brain regions.
keyPoints: Common pathogenesis involves hyperperfusion due to endothelial dysfunction.
Vasogenic edema is the primary process causing neurologic dysfunction.
Disorders include hypertensive encephalopathy, eclampsia, postcarotid endarterectomy syndrome, and calcineurin inhibitor toxicity.
Clinical presentation includes headaches, seizures, or focal neurologic deficits.
Cortical visual loss is typical due to occipital lobe involvement.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · Wernicke's Disease | HYPERPERFUSION DISORDERS (POSTERIOR REVERSIBLE ENCEPHALOPATHY SYNDROME)

items.subtopic: Wernicke's Disease | HYPERPERFUSION DISORDERS (POSTERIOR REVERSIBLE ENCEPHALOPATHY SYNDROME)
items.title: Management of Hyperperfusion Syndromes
items.content: Manage hyperperfusion syndromes by lowering blood pressure, removing offending agents, and treating underlying conditions.
keyPoints: Lower blood pressure judiciously with IV agents like labetalol or nicardipine.
Remove the offending medication if applicable.
Treat underlying conditions such as eclampsia.
Initial MAP reduction by ~20% is reasonable in cases of very elevated BP to prevent secondary ischemia.
Control seizures, using magnesium sulfate for eclampsia.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · Wernicke's Disease | HYPERPERFUSION DISORDERS (POSTERIOR REVERSIBLE ENCEPHALOPATHY SYNDROME)

items.subtopic: POST-SOLID ORGAN TRANSPLANT BRAIN INJURY
items.title: Neurotoxicity from Immunosuppressive Medications in Transplant Patients
items.content: Patients receiving high doses of immunosuppressive medications post-solid organ transplant are at risk for neurologic complications. Specifically, calcineurin inhibitors like cyclosporine and tacrolimus can cause hyperperfusion syndrome even with normal serum levels. Treatment involves reducing or discontinuing the drug. Sirolimus is a potential alternative due to its lower incidence of neurotoxicity.
keyPoints: Calcineurin inhibitors (cyclosporine, tacrolimus) are associated with hyperperfusion syndrome and other neurologic complications.
Neurotoxicity can occur even at normal serum drug levels.
Treatment involves lowering the dosage or discontinuing the medication.
Sirolimus has fewer cases of neurotoxicity and may be a reasonable alternative.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · POST-SOLID ORGAN TRANSPLANT BRAIN INJURY

items.subtopic: POST-SOLID ORGAN TRANSPLANT BRAIN INJURY
items.title: Cerebrovascular Complications in Solid Organ Transplant Patients
items.content: Cerebrovascular complications are common postoperatively, with border zone infarctions and embolic events being significant concerns. Systemic hypotension during cardiac transplant surgery can lead to these issues. Renal or liver transplant patients may require evaluation for right-to-left shunting using agitated saline echocardiography if cerebral embolization is suspected. Imaging (CT/MRI) is crucial to confirm cerebrovascular complications and exclude intracerebral hemorrhage, especially in the context of coagulopathy.
keyPoints: Border zone territory infarctions can occur due to systemic hypotension during cardiac transplant surgery.
Embolic infarctions are common post-cardiac transplantation but all solid organ transplants pose a risk for systemic emboli.
Evaluation for right-to-left shunting may involve agitated saline echocardiography (bubble study).
Imaging with CT or MRI is necessary to confirm cerebrovascular complications and exclude intracerebral hemorrhage.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · POST-SOLID ORGAN TRANSPLANT BRAIN INJURY

items.subtopic: CNS COMPLICATIONS OF CHECKPOINT INHIBITOR AND CHIMERIC ANTIGEN RECEPTOR T-CELL THERAPY
items.title: Neurologic Toxicity of Checkpoint Inhibitors
items.content: Checkpoint inhibitors, used in cancer immunotherapy, can cause neurologic toxicity manifesting as encephalopathy, cerebral edema, or demyelination. These side effects occur due to an autoimmune process triggered by shared antigens between tumor and self.
keyPoints: Neurologic side effects occur in ~5% of patients with monotherapy and ~10% with combination therapy.
Common CNS adverse events include limbic encephalitis, cerebellitis, myelitis, encephalopathy, memory disturbances, seizures.
Peripheral nervous system complications like myasthenia gravis, myositis, neuropathy may be more common than CNS manifestations.
MRI of brain or spinal cord and EEG are recommended for patients with neurologic symptoms.
Treatment involves discontinuing the checkpoint inhibitor and administering high-dose glucocorticoids; intravenous immunoglobulins and plasmapheresis in severe cases.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · CNS COMPLICATIONS OF CHECKPOINT INHIBITOR AND CHIMERIC ANTIGEN RECEPTOR T-CELL THERAPY

items.subtopic: CNS COMPLICATIONS OF CHECKPOINT INHIBITOR AND CHIMERIC ANTIGEN RECEPTOR T-CELL THERAPY
items.title: CAR-T Therapy Neurotoxicity Management
items.content: CAR-T therapy can lead to neurotoxicity, characterized by headache, encephalopathy, aphasia, seizures, and cerebral edema. This occurs in more than half of treated patients and is often associated with cytokine release syndrome (CRS).
keyPoints: Neurotoxicity occurs in >50% of CAR-T-treated patients.
Clinical manifestations include headache, encephalopathy, aphasia, seizures, tremors, cerebral edema.
Predictors of neurotoxicity: earlier/severe CRS, fever, elevated C-reactive protein and serum ferritin, older age.
Treatment involves tocilizumab for CRS, glucocorticoids, brain imaging, EEG if indicated, treatment of cerebral edema and seizures.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · CNS COMPLICATIONS OF CHECKPOINT INHIBITOR AND CHIMERIC ANTIGEN RECEPTOR T-CELL THERAPY

items.subtopic: CNS COMPLICATIONS OF CHECKPOINT INHIBITOR AND CHIMERIC ANTIGEN RECEPTOR T-CELL THERAPY
items.title: Differentiating Neurotoxicity from Other CNS Pathologies in Immunotherapy
items.content: Before diagnosing checkpoint inhibitor-related neurotoxicity, it is crucial to rule out alternative diagnoses such as cerebral metastases, infection, or stroke due to the immunocompromised state of patients.
keyPoints: Rule out cerebral metastases, infections, or stroke in checkpoint inhibitor-treated patients with CNS symptoms.
MRI and CSF analysis (e.g., lymphocytic pleocytosis) are important diagnostic tools.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · CNS COMPLICATIONS OF CHECKPOINT INHIBITOR AND CHIMERIC ANTIGEN RECEPTOR T-CELL THERAPY

items.subtopic: CRITICAL CARE DISORDERS OF THE PERIPHERAL NERVOUS SYSTEM | NEUROPATHY
items.title: Indications for Endotracheal Intubation in PNS Disorders
items.content: In patients with peripheral nervous system (PNS) disorders, endotracheal intubation should be considered when specific respiratory mechanics thresholds are met. These include a maximal inspiratory force (MIF) below -25 cm H2O or a vital capacity (VC) less than 1 L. Additionally, severe palatal weakness may necessitate intubation to prevent airway obstruction or aspiration.
keyPoints: Consider endotracheal intubation when MIF < -25 cm H2O
Consider endotracheal intubation when VC < 1 L
Severe palatal weakness may require intubation to prevent airway complications
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · CRITICAL CARE DISORDERS OF THE PERIPHERAL NERVOUS SYSTEM | NEUROPATHY

items.subtopic: CRITICAL CARE DISORDERS OF THE PERIPHERAL NERVOUS SYSTEM | NEUROPATHY
items.title: Recognition and Management of Critical Illness Polyneuropathy
items.content: Critical illness polyneuropathy is a common PNS complication in prolonged critical illnesses involving sepsis and multisystem organ failure. It presents with diffuse weakness, decreased reflexes, and distal sensory loss. Up to 70% of patients with sepsis may have some degree of neuropathy, but severe cases requiring mechanical ventilation are less frequent. Aggressive glycemic control can reduce the risk.
keyPoints: Common in prolonged critical illness with sepsis
Characterized by diffuse weakness, decreased reflexes, distal sensory loss
Up to 70% of septic patients may have neuropathy
Aggressive glycemic control reduces risk
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · CRITICAL CARE DISORDERS OF THE PERIPHERAL NERVOUS SYSTEM | NEUROPATHY

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF NEUROMUSCULAR TRANSMISSION
items.title: Botulism: Early Recognition and Management in Critically Ill Patients
items.content: Botulism can present with early signs of diplopia and dysphagia, especially following ingestion of botulinum toxin from improperly stored food or gut-derived Clostridium infection. Prompt administration of antitoxin may limit the duration of neuromuscular blockade.
keyPoints: Early signs include diplopia and dysphagia
Antitoxin can be used early to limit neuromuscular blockade duration
Public health notification is crucial for preventing further exposure
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · DISORDERS OF NEUROMUSCULAR TRANSMISSION

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF NEUROMUSCULAR TRANSMISSION
items.title: Prolonged Neuromuscular Blockade: Causes and Monitoring in ICU Patients
items.content: Persistent weakness due to impaired neuromuscular transmission is often drug-induced, particularly by nondepolarizing neuromuscular blocking agents (nd-NMBAs) like pancuronium, vecuronium, rocuronium, and cisatracurium. Risk factors for prolonged action include female sex, metabolic acidosis, and renal failure.
keyPoints: Common drugs causing blockade: aminoglycosides, beta-blockers, nd-NMBAs
Risk factors for prolonged blockade: female sex, metabolic acidosis, renal failure
Use peripheral nerve stimulator to monitor neuromuscular function
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · DISORDERS OF NEUROMUSCULAR TRANSMISSION

items.subtopic: MYOPATHY
items.title: Understanding Critical Illness Myopathy and Its Clinical Implications
items.content: Critical illness myopathy encompasses various muscle disorders in critically ill patients, often associated with sepsis. Cachectic myopathy presents with normal serum creatine kinase levels and EMG but shows type II fiber atrophy on biopsy. Acute necrotizing intensive care myopathy progresses rapidly to profound weakness, with potential elevations in serum creatine kinase and urine myoglobin.
keyPoints: Cachectic myopathy: Normal serum creatine kinase and EMG; muscle biopsy shows type II fiber atrophy.
Acute necrotizing intensive care myopathy: Rapid progression to profound weakness; possible elevated serum creatine kinase and urine myoglobin.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · MYOPATHY

items.subtopic: MYOPATHY
items.title: Thick-Filament Myopathy in Critically Ill Patients: Causes, Prognosis, and Management
items.content: Thick-filament myopathy often occurs with glucocorticoid and nd-NMBA use, particularly in asthmatic patients requiring mechanical ventilation. It results from actual muscle damage rather than prolonged neuromuscular blockade. Clinically, it manifests as difficulty weaning from mechanical ventilation despite resolution of the primary pulmonary issue.
keyPoints: Thick-filament myopathy: Associated with glucocorticoid and nd-NMBA use; characterized by loss of thick (myosin) filaments on pathology.
Prognosis: Generally good, but recovery can take weeks to months; some patients may experience residual weakness and fatigue.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · MYOPATHY

items.subtopic: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care
items.question: Which of the following is a focus of neurologic critical care?
options: Preservation of neurologic tissue and prevention of secondary brain injury.
Treatment of primary systemic disorders like hepatic failure.
Management of cardiac arrest without regard to neurological outcomes.
Prevention of encephalopathy in patients with no prior history.
items.explanation: Neurologic critical care focuses on preservation of neurologic tissue and prevention of secondary brain injury caused by ischemia, hemorrhage, edema, herniation, and elevated ICP.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care

items.subtopic: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care
items.question: What is encephalopathy?
options: A specific type of primary brain tumor.
Brain dysfunction that can be diffuse, global, or multifocal.
An isolated neurologic disorder affecting only the spinal cord.
A systemic disorder primarily affecting liver function.
items.explanation: Encephalopathy is a general term describing brain dysfunction that is diffuse, global, or multifocal.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care

items.subtopic: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care
items.question: Which of the following can cause life-threatening neurologic illness?
options: Primary disorders affecting any region of the neuraxis.
Exclusively cardiac arrest without other complications.
Only systemic disorders like hepatic failure.
Isolated secondary brain injury without primary causes.
items.explanation: Life-threatening neurologic illness may be caused by a primary disorder affecting any region of the neuraxis or as a consequence of a systemic disorder such as hepatic failure, multisystem organ failure, or cardiac arrest.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care

items.subtopic: ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)
items.question: Which type of edema is characterized by the influx of fluid through an incompetent blood-brain barrier?
items.explanation: Vasogenic edema refers to the influx of fluid and solutes into the brain through a compromised blood-brain barrier.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)
items.question: What is the primary cause of cytotoxic edema?
options: Influx of calcium and sodium ions
items.explanation: Cytotoxic edema results from cellular swelling due to the influx of calcium and sodium ions, disrupting cellular homeostasis.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)
items.question: Which condition is NOT typically associated with apoptotic cell death?
items.explanation: Apoptosis does not typically occur with cerebral edema, which is more associated with necrotic cell death.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)
items.question: What physiological mechanism helps maintain constant cerebral blood flow despite changes in systemic blood pressure?
items.explanation: Cerebral autoregulation adjusts cerebrovascular resistance to maintain constant cerebral blood flow across varying systemic pressures.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a cause for increased intracranial pressure (ICP)?
options: Increases in volume from tumor or hematoma
items.explanation: Increased ICP is caused by obstruction of CSF outflow, edema, and increases in volume from tumors or hematomas, not decreased cerebral blood volume.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.subtopic: ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)
items.question: What is the primary consequence of elevated intracranial pressure?
items.explanation: Elevated ICP diminishes cerebral perfusion, potentially leading to tissue ischemia.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.subtopic: ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)
items.question: Which of the following conditions is NOT commonly associated with a vicious cycle involving increased ICP and ischemia?
options: Large hemispheric infarcts with significant tissue shifts
items.explanation: The vicious cycle of increased ICP and ischemia is commonly seen in traumatic brain injury, massive intracerebral hemorrhage, and large hemispheric infarcts, not benign positional vertigo.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.subtopic: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Severe Brain Dysfunction (part 1)
items.question: What is the primary goal when initially evaluating a critically ill patient with severe CNS dysfunction?
options: To determine if the cause of dysfunction is related to a diffuse or focal process.
To immediately perform an EEG on all patients.
To administer antibiotics empirically before any diagnostic studies.
To assess only respiratory and cardiac parameters.
items.explanation: The primary goal is to determine whether the cause of dysfunction is related to a diffuse, usually metabolic process or a focal, usually structural, process.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Severe Brain Dysfunction (part 1)

items.subtopic: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Severe Brain Dysfunction (part 1)
items.question: Which neuroimaging study is typically preferred as the initial evaluation for critically ill patients with decreased consciousness?
items.explanation: Computed tomography (CT) scanning is usually the most appropriate initial study because it can be performed quickly and demonstrates hemorrhage, hydrocephalus, and intracranial tissue shifts well.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Severe Brain Dysfunction (part 1)

items.subtopic: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Severe Brain Dysfunction (part 1)
items.question: In which scenario should a noncontrast CT scan of the brain be urgently performed?
options: If there are brief episodes of spontaneous extensor posturing with cranial nerve abnormalities.
For all patients with coma of unknown etiology.
items.explanation: A noncontrast CT scan should be performed urgently in cases of sudden onset coma accompanied by brief episodes of spontaneous extensor posturing and cranial nerve abnormalities, suggesting acute brainstem ischemia.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Severe Brain Dysfunction (part 1)

items.subtopic: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Severe Brain Dysfunction (part 1)
items.question: Which diagnostic study is most useful for excluding inapparent seizures in critically ill patients?
items.explanation: Electroencephalography (EEG) is important for excluding inapparent seizures, especially nonconvulsive status epilepticus.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Severe Brain Dysfunction (part 1)

items.subtopic: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Severe Brain Dysfunction (part 2) | TREATMENT OF ELEVATED ICP
items.question: Which patients are generally considered for ICP monitoring?
options: Patients with mild traumatic brain injury and GCS score >8
Patients with primary neurologic disorders at risk for secondary brain injury due to elevated ICP
Patients with stable intracranial pressure and no history of neurological issues
Patients undergoing routine physical examinations without any neurological symptoms
items.explanation: ICP monitoring is considered for patients with primary neurologic disorders at risk for secondary brain injury due to elevated ICP.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Severe Brain Dysfunction (part 2) | TREATMENT OF ELEVATED ICP

items.subtopic: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Severe Brain Dysfunction (part 2) | TREATMENT OF ELEVATED ICP
items.question: What is the preferred method of ICP monitoring in patients with large ventricles?
items.explanation: Ventriculosome is preferable because it allows CSF drainage, which is a method of treating elevated ICP.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Severe Brain Dysfunction (part 2) | TREATMENT OF ELEVATED ICP

items.subtopic: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Severe Brain Dysfunction (part 2) | TREATMENT OF ELEVATED ICP
items.question: In fulminant hepatic failure with elevated ICP, what treatment options are considered?
items.explanation: In fulminant hepatic failure with elevated ICP, barbiturates or eventually liver transplantation can be considered.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT Severe Brain Dysfunction (part 2) | TREATMENT OF ELEVATED ICP

items.question: What is the initial step recommended for managing elevated ICP if it exceeds 20–25 mmHg for more than 5 minutes?

items.question: Which osmotic agent can be used to manage elevated ICP, and what is the maximum serum osmolality that should be maintained?

items.question: What is the target arterial partial pressure of carbon dioxide ($ Paco_{2} $) when using hyperventilation as a treatment for elevated ICP?

items.subtopic: SECONDARY BRAIN INSULTS
items.question: Which clinical event is NOT typically associated with episodes of secondary brain insults?
items.explanation: Episodes of secondary brain insults are usually not associated with apparent neurologic worsening.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · SECONDARY BRAIN INSULTS

items.subtopic: SECONDARY BRAIN INSULTS
items.question: What is the recommended goal for serum glucose level management in patients at risk for secondary brain injury?
items.explanation: A reasonable goal is to maintain the serum glucose level at <10.0 mmol/L (<180 mg/dL).
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · SECONDARY BRAIN INSULTS

items.subtopic: SECONDARY BRAIN INSULTS
items.question: Which of the following interventions is encouraged for controlling hyperglycemia in patients with secondary brain injury?
items.explanation: The use of IV insulin infusion is encouraged for control of hyperglycemia because this allows better regulation of serum glucose levels.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · SECONDARY BRAIN INSULTS

items.subtopic: HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following conditions can cause hypoxic-ischemic brain injury due to direct impairment of the respiratory chain?
items.explanation: Carbon monoxide and cyanide poisonings are termed histotoxic hypoxia because they directly impair the respiratory chain.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 1)

items.subtopic: HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 1)
items.question: What is a poor prognostic sign in patients with hypoxic-ischemic brain injury?
options: Absence of pupillary light reflex or extensor motor response to pain 5–7 days post-injury
Presence of N20 component in SSEP
items.explanation: An absent pupillary light reflex or extensor or absent motor response to pain 5–7 days following the injury is a poor prognostic sign.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 1)

items.subtopic: HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 1)
items.question: What is indicated by an elevated serum level of neuron-specific enolase (NSE) after cardiac arrest?
options: Worse outcome due to brain damage
items.explanation: A very elevated serum level of NSE (>60 µg/L) indicates brain damage after resuscitation from cardiac arrest and is associated with a worse outcome.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 1)

items.subtopic: HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 1)
items.question: Which condition may be explained by selective damage to the hippocampus following brief hypoxia-ischemia?
items.explanation: The vulnerability of hippocampal CA1 neurons to brief hypoxia-ischemia may explain selective persistent memory deficits.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 1)

items.subtopic: HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 2) | Hypoxic-Ischemic Brain Injury
items.question: Which of the following is a recommended temperature range for postarrest temperature control in patients with hypoxic-ischemic brain injury?
items.explanation: Current guidelines recommend maintaining a constant temperature between 32 and 37.5°C during postarrest temperature control.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 2) | Hypoxic-Ischemic Brain Injury

items.subtopic: HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 2) | Hypoxic-Ischemic Brain Injury
items.question: What is the suggested clinical finding that may indicate carbon monoxide intoxication?
options: Cherry red color of venous blood and skin
items.explanation: Carbon monoxide intoxication can be suggested by a cherry red color of the venous blood and skin.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 2) | Hypoxic-Ischemic Brain Injury

items.subtopic: HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 2) | Hypoxic-Ischemic Brain Injury
items.question: Which treatment has been found to improve functional outcomes in some clinical trials for patients comatose after cardiac arrest?
items.explanation: Several clinical trials found that mild hypothermia (33°C) administered for 12–24 h improved functional outcome in patients who remained comatose after resuscitation from cardiac arrest.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 2) | Hypoxic-Ischemic Brain Injury

items.subtopic: HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 2) | Hypoxic-Ischemic Brain Injury
items.question: What is a potential complication of hypothermia treatment in patients with hypoxic-ischemic brain injury?
items.explanation: Potential complications of hypothermia include systemic coagulopathy and an increased risk of infection.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC BRAIN INJURY (part 2) | Hypoxic-Ischemic Brain Injury

items.subtopic: POSTCARDIAC BYPASS BRAIN INJURY
items.question: Which imaging modality has provided evidence that embolic disease is likely the predominant mechanism of cerebral injury during cardiac surgery?
items.explanation: Diffusion-weighted MRI has shown evidence supporting embolic disease as a primary mechanism of cerebral injury during cardiac surgery.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · POSTCARDIAC BYPASS BRAIN INJURY

items.subtopic: POSTCARDIAC BYPASS BRAIN INJURY
items.question: What is often incorrectly attributed to depression or sedative-induced delirium in the context of postoperative complications after cardiac surgery?
options: Acute encephalopathy presenting as a hypoactive confusional state
Large-vessel stroke with clinical focal deficits
Chronic cognitive deficit without acute syndrome
Systemic hypotension leading to border zone infarcts
items.explanation: Acute encephalopathy presenting as a hypoactive confusional state is often incorrectly attributed to depression or sedative-induced delirium.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · POSTCARDIAC BYPASS BRAIN INJURY

items.subtopic: POSTCARDIAC BYPASS BRAIN INJURY
items.question: Which of the following has NOT been implicated as a potential source of emboli during cardiac surgery?

items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · POSTCARDIAC BYPASS BRAIN INJURY

items.subtopic: METABOLIC ENCEPHALOPATHIES
items.question: Which medication may reduce delirium and shorten the duration of mechanical ventilation in critically ill patients requiring sedation compared to benzodiazepines?
items.explanation: Dexmedetomidine, a centrally acting $ \alpha_{2} $ agonist, may reduce delirium and shorten the duration of mechanical ventilation compared to benzodiazepines.

items.question: Which condition is suggested by asterixis in patients with hepatic encephalopathy?
items.explanation: Asterixis is a sign of hepatic encephalopathy, which can occur in both chronic liver failure and acute fulminant hepatic failure.

items.question: What are the hallmarks of acute Wernicke's disease?

items.subtopic: SEPSIS-ASSOCIATED ENCEPHALOPATHY
items.question: Which cytokines are thought to play a role in sepsis-associated encephalopathy?
options: Tumor necrosis factor, interleukin (IL) 1, IL-2, and IL-6
C-reactive protein, fibrinogen, and serum amyloid A
Erythropoietin, granulocyte colony-stimulating factor, and thrombopoietin
items.explanation: Tumor necrosis factor, IL-1, IL-2, and IL-6 are implicated in the pathogenesis of sepsis-associated encephalopathy.

items.question: What is a common clinical presentation of sepsis-associated encephalopathy?

items.subtopic: SEPSIS-ASSOCIATED ENCEPHALOPATHY
items.question: What is the mortality rate for patients with sepsis-associated encephalopathy severe enough to produce coma?

$\leq$10 mmol/L (10 meq/L) within 12 hours and $\leq$20 mmol/L (20 meq/L) within 36 hours
Immediate correction to normal sodium levels over 6 hours
No specific rate, as long as symptoms are monitored

Demyelination without inflammation at the base of the pons
Axonal damage with nerve cell loss in the brainstem
Vascular occlusion leading to ischemia in the cerebellum
items.explanation: Osmotic demyelination syndrome involves demyelination without inflammation in the base of the pons.

items.question: What is a common clinical presentation of osmotic demyelination syndrome?

Sudden loss of consciousness with seizures
items.explanation: Osmotic demyelination syndrome often presents as quadriplegia and pseudobulbar palsy.

items.assertion: The rapidity of rise in blood pressure is a critical risk factor for hyperperfusion disorders.
items.reason: Hyperperfusion disorders are caused by exceeding the cerebral autoregulatory threshold, leading to increased CBF and capillary leakage.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; rapid rise in pressure can exceed autoregulatory thresholds.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · Wernicke's Disease | HYPERPERFUSION DISORDERS (POSTERIOR REVERSIBLE ENCEPHALOPATHY SYNDROME)

items.subtopic: CNS COMPLICATIONS OF CHECKPOINT INHIBITOR AND CHIMERIC ANTIGEN RECEPTOR T-CELL THERAPY
items.assertion: Checkpoint inhibitor-related neurotoxicity should be diagnosed after ruling out alternative diagnoses.
items.reason: Patients treated with checkpoint inhibitors are immunocompromised, necessitating the exclusion of other potential causes like infection or stroke before confirming neurotoxicity.
items.explanation: It is crucial to rule out cerebral metastases, infections, or strokes in immunocompromised patients before diagnosing checkpoint inhibitor-related neurotoxicity.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · CNS COMPLICATIONS OF CHECKPOINT INHIBITOR AND CHIMERIC ANTIGEN RECEPTOR T-CELL THERAPY

items.subtopic: CNS COMPLICATIONS OF CHECKPOINT INHIBITOR AND CHIMERIC ANTIGEN RECEPTOR T-CELL THERAPY
items.assertion: CAR-T therapy is associated with cytokine release syndrome (CRS) and neurotoxicity.
items.reason: Both CRS and neurotoxicity are side effects of CAR-T therapy, often occurring within days of treatment initiation.
items.explanation: CAR-T therapy frequently causes both CRS and neurotoxicity, which can occur concurrently in the same patient.
items.reference: Nervous System Disorders in Critical Care · CNS COMPLICATIONS OF CHECKPOINT INHIBITOR AND CHIMERIC ANTIGEN RECEPTOR T-CELL THERAPY
